svg.text: What "shortest path" means with negative cycles
If a negative cycle is reachable from s and can reach v, then dist[s, v] = -∞: keep going
around the cycle. Bellman-Ford detects this on the Vth pass — any edge still relaxing
indicates a vertex on or downstream of a negative cycle.
To extract the cycle: take any vertex v relaxed on the Vth pass, walk parent pointers
V times to land on the cycle, then walk parents until you revisit a node.
Negative cycles are useful: arbitrage detection, infeasibility proofs, LP duals.
1. Add a fake source q with 0-weight edges to every vertex.
2. Run Bellman-Ford from q to compute h[v] = dist(q, v). If neg cycle → fail.
3. Reweight each edge: w'(u, v) = w(u, v) + h[u] - h[v]. Provably non-negative.
4. Run Dijkstra from each vertex on reweighted graph.
5. Recover original distances: d(u, v) = d'(u, v) - h[u] + h[v].
Total: O(VE + V·(V+E) log V). Beats Floyd-Warshall O(V³) on sparse graphs.
Reweighting also explains why A* works — it's a vertex potential function.
